questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which statement correctly describes the octet rule and its limitations?

options.A: All elements must have exactly eight valence electrons to be stable.
options.B: Most main-group elements are stable with eight valence electrons, but hydrogen and helium are stable with two.
options.C: Only noble gases follow the octet rule; all other elements violate it.
options.D: The octet rule applies only to ionic compounds, not covalent compounds.

questions.hint: Remember that the first energy level can only hold two electrons.
questions.explanation: The octet rule (eight valence electrons) applies to most main-group elements. Hydrogen and helium are exceptions because their first energy level (n=1) holds a maximum of two electrons (duet rule).

source_trace.content_anchor: The octet rule states that atoms tend to form compounds in ways that give them eight valence electrons. Hydrogen and helium need only two electrons

explanations_tiered.definition: The octet rule states that atoms tend to form compounds that give them eight valence electrons, achieving a noble gas configuration.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Most main-group elements follow the octet rule. Hydrogen and helium are exceptions because they only need two electrons (duet rule) to fill their first energy level.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Most atoms want 8 outer electrons to be happy, but hydrogen and helium are tiny — their parking lot only fits 2 electrons.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The octet rule applies to period 2 and beyond (\(ns^2np^6\)). Period 1 has only the 1s orbital (capacity 2), so H and He follow the duet rule.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The octet rule arises from quantum mechanical stability of filled s and p subshells. For n=1, only the 1s subshell exists, with a capacity of 2 electrons.
reasoning_steps: Recall octet rule: 8 valence electrons for stability.
Recognize that n=1 can only hold 2 electrons (1s²).
Thus H and He are stable with 2 electrons (duet rule).
misconceptions: Some think all elements need 8 electrons. Hydrogen and helium are exceptions due to n=1 capacity limit.
Another misconception: noble gases don't follow the octet rule. Helium follows duet rule; other noble gases follow octet rule.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Octet (8 e⁻) for most elements; duet (2 e⁻) for H and He.

glossary_entry.definition: Hydrogen and helium are stable with 2 electrons in their valence shell (the 1s orbital).

flashcard.front: What is the exception to the octet rule for hydrogen and helium?
flashcard.back: They follow the duet rule, stable with 2 electrons (1s²).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the octet rule and its exception?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Most elements stable with 8 valence electrons; H and He stable with 2 (duet rule).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Hydrogen and helium are stable with 2 electrons in their valence shell (the 1s orbital).
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Octet (8 e⁻) for most; duet (2 e⁻) for H and He.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that the exception is due to the capacity of the first energy level.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the octet rule and its exceptions
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Most elements follow octet (8 e⁻); H and He follow duet (2 e⁻). A is false (exceptions exist). C is false (many non-noble gases follow octet). D is false (octet applies to covalent too).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.subsection: How could atoms satisfy the Octet Rule?
questions.structural_anchor: How could atoms satisfy the Octet Rule?
questions.question: A sulfur atom (S) has six valence electrons. To satisfy the octet rule, sulfur can either gain two electrons or share two electrons. In which type of compound does sulfur gain electrons, and in which type does it share electrons?

options.A: Gains in ionic compounds (e.g., Na₂S); shares in covalent compounds (e.g., H₂S)
options.B: Shares in ionic compounds; gains in covalent compounds
options.C: Always gains electrons regardless of compound type
options.D: Always shares electrons regardless of compound type

questions.hint: Consider whether sulfur is bonding with a metal or a nonmetal.
questions.explanation: In ionic compounds, sulfur gains two electrons to become \(S^{2-}\) (e.g., Na₂S). In covalent compounds, sulfur shares electrons with nonmetals to complete its octet (e.g., H₂S, where S shares with two H atoms).

source_trace.subsection_anchor: How could atoms satisfy the Octet Rule?
source_trace.structural_element: How could atoms satisfy the Octet Rule?
source_trace.content_anchor: By losing their own valence electrons, gaining valence electrons from other elements, or sharing their valence electrons

explanations_tiered.definition: Nonmetals can achieve octet by gaining electrons (forming anions) in ionic bonds or by sharing electrons in covalent bonds.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Sulfur (Group 16) needs 2 electrons. With metals (e.g., Na), it gains 2 e⁻ to form \(S^{2-}\) (ionic). With nonmetals (e.g., H), it shares electrons (covalent).
explanations_tiered.beginner: With metals like sodium, sulfur takes two electrons (ionic). With other nonmetals like hydrogen, sulfur shares electrons (covalent).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Sulfur (6 valence e⁻) can achieve octet by gaining 2 e⁻ (ionic: Na₂S → S²⁻) or by forming two covalent bonds (H₂S, SF₆ etc.).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The electronegativity difference determines bond type. With metals (ΔEN large), electron transfer occurs. With nonmetals (ΔEN moderate), covalent sharing occurs.
reasoning_steps: Identify sulfur's valence electrons: 6, needs 2 more.
With metal (Na): metal loses e⁻, S gains e⁻ → ionic.
With nonmetal (H): both nonmetals share e⁻ → covalent.
misconceptions: Some think a given element always uses the same bonding method. Sulfur can gain (ionic) or share (covalent) depending on the other element.
Another misconception: sulfur only forms covalent bonds because it's a nonmetal. Sulfur forms ionic sulfides with metals.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: S (needs 2 e⁻): gains (ionic) with metals; shares (covalent) with nonmetals.

glossary_entry.definition: The anion \(S^{2-}\) formed when sulfur gains two electrons in ionic bonding.

flashcard.front: How does sulfur achieve octet in Na₂S vs H₂S?
flashcard.back: Na₂S: ionic, S gains 2 e⁻ to become \(S^{2-}\). H₂S: covalent, S shares 2 e⁻ with two H atoms.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Does sulfur gain or share electrons in Na₂S vs H₂S?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The anion \(S^{2-}\) formed when sulfur gains two electrons in ionic bonding.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: S: gains e⁻ with metals; shares e⁻ with nonmetals.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast Na₂S (ionic, S²⁻) with H₂S (covalent, sharing).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Distinguish between ionic and covalent bonding for the same element
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. S gains electrons in ionic compounds with metals (Na₂S); shares electrons in covalent compounds with nonmetals (H₂S). B is opposite. C and D are false (depends on bonding partner).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.sub_subsection: The Distinction between Polar and Non-polar Covalent Bond and Ionic Bond
questions.structural_anchor: The Distinction between Polar and Non-polar Covalent Bond and Ionic Bond
questions.question: Using electronegativity values (H=2.1, Cl=3.0, Na=0.9, F=4.0), classify the bonds in HCl, NaF, and Cl₂.
questions.hint: Calculate ΔEN: ≥2.0 = ionic, 0.5–2.0 = polar covalent, <0.5 = nonpolar covalent.
questions.explanation: ΔEN: HCl = 3.0−2.1=0.9 (polar covalent); NaF = 4.0−0.9=3.1 (ionic); Cl₂ = 3.0−3.0=0 (nonpolar covalent). Option B matches these classifications.

source_trace.structural_element: The Distinction between Polar and Non-polar Covalent Bond and Ionic Bond
source_trace.content_anchor: An ionic bond results when the electronegativity difference is 2.0 or more. A polar covalent bond forms when the difference is in the range 0.5-2.0

explanations_tiered.definition: Bond type is classified by electronegativity difference: ionic ≥ 2.0, polar covalent 0.5–2.0, nonpolar covalent <0.5.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: HCl: ΔEN=0.9 → polar covalent. NaF: ΔEN=3.1 → ionic. Cl₂: ΔEN=0 → nonpolar covalent.
explanations_tiered.beginner: HCl difference (0.9) is medium → polar covalent. NaF difference (3.1) is big → ionic. Cl₂ difference (0) is zero → nonpolar.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Calculate ΔEN: HCl = 3.0−2.1=0.9 (polar covalent range). NaF = 4.0−0.9=3.1 (ionic range). Cl₂ = 3.0−3.0=0 (nonpolar).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Percent ionic character: NaF ~90% (ionic), HCl ~17% (polar covalent), Cl₂ 0% (nonpolar covalent).
reasoning_steps: Calculate ΔEN for each bond.
Compare to thresholds: <0.5 nonpolar, 0.5–2.0 polar, ≥2.0 ionic.
Match classifications.
misconceptions: Some think HCl is ionic because it conducts electricity in water. HCl is covalent; it ionizes in water but is not ionic as a pure substance.
Another misconception: Cl₂ is polar because chlorine is electronegative. Identical atoms have ΔEN=0 → nonpolar.

glossary_entry.definition: The difference in electronegativity values between two bonded atoms, used to predict bond type.

flashcard.back: HCl: polar covalent; NaF: ionic; Cl₂: nonpolar covalent.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Classify HCl, NaF, and Cl₂ using electronegativity differences.
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: HCl: polar covalent (0.9); NaF: ionic (3.1); Cl₂: nonpolar (0).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The difference in electronegativity values between two bonded atoms, used to predict bond type.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Ensure students calculate ΔEN correctly and apply thresholds consistently.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Distinguish bond types using electronegativity differences
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. ΔEN: HCl 0.9 (polar covalent), NaF 3.1 (ionic), Cl₂ 0 (nonpolar). A misclassifies HCl and NaF. C misclassifies HCl and Cl₂. D misclassifies all three.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: According to the 'sea of electrons' model, why are metals malleable (can be hammered into sheets) while ionic crystals are brittle?

options.A: Metal cations are larger and can move past each other without repulsion.
options.B: Delocalized electrons in metals allow cations to slide while maintaining bonding; shifting layers in ionic crystals brings like charges together, causing repulsion and fracture.
options.C: Metals have lower melting points, so they flow more easily.
options.D: Ionic crystals have covalent bonds within their lattice that break upon impact.

questions.hint: Contrast the electron sea with alternating +/− layers in ionic crystals.
questions.explanation: In metals, delocalized electrons act as a 'glue' that allows cations to slide past each other without breaking bonding. In ionic crystals, shifting layers aligns like charges, causing repulsion and shattering (brittleness).

source_trace.content_anchor: The electrons freely move within these molecular outermost shells, and therefore each electron becomes detached from its parent atom. The electrons are said to be delocalized

explanations_tiered.definition: Malleability is the ability to be hammered into thin sheets; ductility is the ability to be drawn into wires.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Metals: delocalized electrons allow cations to slide without losing bonding. Ionic crystals: layers of alternating charges; shifting brings like charges together → repulsion → fracture.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Metal ions slide like magnets on a metal sheet — the 'electron sea' keeps them stuck together. Ionic crystals are like alternating red and black squares — if you push, red touches red and they push apart violently.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In metals, the nondirectional electron sea maintains bonding during layer displacement. In ionic crystals, displacement creates like-charge repulsion (\(F = k q_1 q_2 / r^2\)), causing brittle fracture.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Metals exhibit plastic deformation via dislocation motion without catastrophic failure. Ionic crystals lack this because dislocation would bring ions of like charge into contact, creating an unstable, high-energy configuration that fractures.
reasoning_steps: Recall metals: delocalized electrons, cations slide.
Recall ionic crystals: alternating + and − ions in layers.
Layer shift in metals: electron sea adjusts, bonding maintained.
Layer shift in ionic crystals: like charges align → repulsion → shatter.
misconceptions: Some think metals are malleable because they are soft. Many metals (e.g., tungsten) are hard but still malleable.
Another misconception: ionic crystals shatter because they are weak. Ionic bonds are strong, but they are brittle due to repulsion on layer shift.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Metals malleable: electron sea allows sliding. Ionic crystals brittle: layer shift aligns like charges → repulsion → shatter.

glossary_entry.definition: The ability of a metal to be hammered into thin sheets without breaking, due to delocalized electrons allowing cation layers to slide.

flashcard.front: Why are metals malleable but ionic crystals brittle?
flashcard.back: Metals: delocalized electrons allow sliding; ionic crystals: shifting layers align like charges, causing repulsion and fracture.

concept_map_nodes.relation: have alternating +/− → shift aligns like charges → repulsion →

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why are metals malleable but ionic compounds are brittle?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Metals: electron sea allows cation sliding. Ionic: shifting layers aligns like charges → repulsion → shatter.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The ability of a metal to be hammered into thin sheets without breaking, due to delocalized electrons allowing cation layers to slide.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Metals: malleable (e⁻ sea). Ionic: brittle (like charge repulsion).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Demonstrate with hammering a metal (flattens) vs hammering an ionic crystal (shatters).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Compare mechanical properties of metals and ionic compounds
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Delocalized electrons in metals allow sliding (B); ionic crystals align like charges on shift → repulsion → shatter. A is false (size not primary). C is false (many metals have high melting points). D is false (ionic crystals have ionic, not covalent, bonds).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Sodium metal (Na) has a melting point of \(98^\circ C\), magnesium (Mg) melts at \(650^\circ C\), and aluminum (Al) melts at \(660^\circ C\). If the melting point trend from Na to Mg to Al is due to increasing numbers of delocalized electrons, what would you predict about the melting point of silicon (Si, group 14)?
questions.hint: Silicon is a metalloid with a covalent network structure, not a metal, so it does not follow the metallic bonding trend.
questions.explanation: Si is not a metal; it has a covalent network structure (diamond-like) with strong covalent bonds in three dimensions. Its melting point (\(1414^\circ C\)) is much higher than metals, not explained by delocalized electrons.

calculation_steps: Recognize that Na, Mg, Al are metals with delocalized electrons (1, 2, 3 e⁻ per atom respectively).
Silicon has a covalent network structure (diamond cubic), not metallic bonding.
Covalent network solids have very high melting points (Si: 1414°C).
Therefore, Si's melting point is higher than Al's.

source_trace.content_anchor: Transition metals tend to have particularly high melting points and boiling points. The reason is that they can involve more valence electrons in the delocalization

explanations_tiered.definition: Melting point trends in period 3: Na (metal, 1 e⁻) → Mg (metal, 2 e⁻) → Al (metal, 3 e⁻) increase, but Si (metalloid, covalent network) has much higher melting point.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Na, Mg, Al are metals with increasing delocalized electrons (1, 2, 3), increasing metallic bond strength. Silicon is a covalent network solid with strong directional covalent bonds, giving a much higher melting point.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The pattern of more free electrons (Na→Mg→Al) increases melting point for metals. But silicon is not a metal — it's like diamond, with a 3D network of strong bonds, so it melts at a much higher temperature.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Na (1 e⁻), Mg (2 e⁻), Al (3 e⁻) show increasing metallic bond strength → increasing m.p. Si has a covalent network (diamond structure) with bond energy ~368 kJ/mol and m.p. 1414°C, much higher than Al.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Silicon (Group 14) has sp³ hybridization and forms four strong covalent bonds per atom in a tetrahedral network. The melting point (1414°C) reflects the energy required to break these directional covalent bonds throughout the crystal.
worked_solution: Step 1: Na, Mg, Al are metals; melting points increase with more delocalized electrons.
Step 2: Si is in Group 14 but is a metalloid with covalent network bonding.
Step 3: Covalent network solids have very high melting points due to strong 3D covalent bonds.
Step 4: Si melts at 1414°C, which is higher than Al (660°C).
reasoning_steps: Identify bonding type for Si: covalent network (diamond cubic).
Recall that covalent network solids have very high melting points.
Therefore, Si melting point > Al melting point.
misconceptions: Some think the trend continues across period 3. Silicon is not a metal; it does not have metallic bonding.
Another misconception: more valence electrons always means higher melting point. This applies within metals but not across to covalent networks.

glossary_entry.definition: A solid in which atoms are bonded together by a continuous network of covalent bonds, giving high melting points and hardness.

flashcard.front: Would you expect silicon's melting point to be higher or lower than aluminum's? Why?
flashcard.back: Higher; silicon is a covalent network solid with strong 3D covalent bonds, unlike metallic Al.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Predict silicon's melting point relative to aluminum. Explain.
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Higher. Si is a covalent network solid (diamond structure), not a metal, so m.p. ~1414°C > Al's 660°C.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A solid in which atoms are bonded together by a continuous network of covalent bonds, giving high melting points and hardness.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Point out that period 3 trends change at Si due to different bonding type.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Predict bonding and properties across a period

concept_map_nodes.relation: Na, Mg, Al (metals) → m.p. increases with e⁻ count

publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. Si is not a metal; it has a covalent network structure (diamond-like) with very strong bonds, melting at 1414°C (>660°C). A, B, C assume Si follows metallic trend, which is incorrect.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Draw the Lewis dot structure for calcium oxide (CaO). Calcium (Group 2) loses two electrons, and oxygen (Group 16) gains two electrons. What is the correct Lewis representation for the ions in CaO?

options.B: \(Ca^{2+} \; [\ddot{O}]^{2-}\) with eight dots (four pairs) around O

questions.hint: Oxygen has 6 valence electrons; gaining 2 gives 8 (octet), shown as 4 pairs of dots.
questions.explanation: O²⁻ has 8 valence electrons (octet), represented in Lewis notation as 4 pairs of dots (or 8 dots) around O. Ca²⁺ has lost its two valence electrons, so it has no dots. The correct Lewis formula is \(Ca^{2+} \; [\ddot{O}]^{2-}\).

source_trace.content_anchor: Lewis symbols can also be used to illustrate the formation of anions from atoms

explanations_tiered.definition: The Lewis dot symbol for an ion shows the chemical symbol with dots representing valence electrons, and the charge as a superscript.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Ca²⁺ has no valence electrons (no dots). O²⁻ has 8 valence electrons (octet), shown as 4 pairs of dots surrounding O.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Calcium gives away its two electrons, so it has no dots left. Oxygen gets those two, so now it has 8 dots (four pairs).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Neutral O has 6 valence electrons (two lone pairs, two unpaired). Gaining 2 electrons gives O²⁻ with 4 lone pairs (8 e⁻). Lewis symbol: \([\ddot{O}]^{2-}\). Ca²⁺: \(Ca^{2+}\) (no dots).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The Lewis structure of CaO is ionic: \(Ca^{2+}\) and \(O^{2-}\) with electron configuration of Ca²⁺ = [Ar], O²⁻ = [Ne] (1s²2s²2p⁶).
reasoning_steps: Determine valence electrons: Ca (2) loses both → Ca²⁺ has 0.
O (6) gains 2 → O²⁻ has 8 valence electrons.
Represent O²⁻ with 4 pairs of dots (octet).
Write as Ca²⁺ and [Ö]²⁻.
misconceptions: Some think anions still show the dots from the neutral atom. O²⁻ has 8 dots (4 pairs), not 6.
Another misconception: cations still have dots. Ca²⁺ has lost all valence electrons, so no dots.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: CaO: Ca²⁺ (no dots), O²⁻ (8 dots: 4 pairs).

glossary_entry.definition: A representation showing the chemical symbol with dots for valence electrons and a superscript for the charge.

flashcard.front: What is the Lewis dot representation for CaO?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Draw the Lewis dot symbols for Ca²⁺ and O²⁻ in CaO.
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Ca²⁺ (no dots), O²⁻ with 8 dots (four pairs).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A representation showing the chemical symbol with dots for valence electrons and a superscript for the charge.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Ca²⁺: no dots; O²⁻: 8 dots (4 pairs).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Practice with MgO, NaCl, etc., to reinforce cation/anion Lewis symbols.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Draw Lewis structures for ionic compounds
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Ca²⁺ has no dots (lost both valence e⁻). O²⁻ gained 2 e⁻, total 8 valence e⁻ → 4 pairs of dots. A has too few dots. C has no charge on Ca. D has no dots on O.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The Lewis structure of methane (CH₄) shows carbon with four single bonds to hydrogen atoms. How many bonding pairs and lone pairs are around the carbon atom?
questions.hint: Carbon has 4 valence electrons and forms 4 bonds; it has no lone pairs in CH₄.
questions.explanation: Carbon has 4 valence electrons. In CH₄, it forms four single bonds with four hydrogen atoms, using all 4 electrons in bonding. No electrons remain to form lone pairs, so carbon has 4 bonding pairs and 0 lone pairs.

source_trace.content_anchor: Each hydrogen atom acquires a helium-like electron configuration. Such a type of bond is known as a single bond

explanations_tiered.definition: Bonding pairs are shared electron pairs; lone pairs are nonbonding valence electrons localized on an atom.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: C has 4 valence e⁻; each C-H bond uses 1 e⁻ from C and 1 e⁻ from H. C uses all 4 e⁻ in four single bonds, leaving 0 e⁻ for lone pairs.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Carbon uses all its 4 electrons to hold hands with 4 hydrogens. No electrons are left over to be a 'private pair'.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Lewis structure of CH₄: C at center with four C-H single bonds. C has 4 bonding pairs and no lone pairs, satisfying the octet rule.
explanations_tiered.advanced: In CH₄, carbon is sp³ hybridized, forming four equivalent σ bonds. The electron configuration after bonding is (2s²2p²) → four sp³ orbitals, each containing one electron for bonding.
reasoning_steps: Count valence electrons of C: 4.
Each single bond = 1 bonding pair = 2 electrons.
Four C-H bonds use all 4 e⁻ from C.
No remaining electrons for lone pairs.
misconceptions: Some think carbon has lone pairs in CH₄ because it has 4 electrons. Those 4 are all used in bonding; none remain.
Another misconception: CH₄ has a lone pair on carbon because of its tetrahedral shape. Lone pairs are nonbonding electrons, not related to shape.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: CH₄: C has 4 bonding pairs, 0 lone pairs.

glossary_entry.definition: A pair of electrons shared between two atoms in a covalent bond.

flashcard.front: How many bonding pairs and lone pairs are around carbon in CH₄?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How many bonding and lone pairs around C in CH₄?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A pair of electrons shared between two atoms in a covalent bond.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: CH₄: 4 bonding pairs, 0 lone pairs on C.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Compare with NH₃ (3 bonding, 1 lone) and H₂O (2 bonding, 2 lone).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Identify bonding and lone pairs in Lewis structures

concept_map_nodes.relation: forms 4 single bonds → uses all e⁻ →

publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. C has 4 valence e⁻, forms 4 single bonds (4 bonding pairs), uses all e⁻ → 0 lone pairs. B (3 bonding, 1 lone) is NH₃. C (2 bonding, 2 lone) is H₂O. D impossible (only 8 e⁻ maximum around C).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Formaldehyde (CH₂O) has a Lewis structure with carbon doubly bonded to oxygen and singly bonded to two hydrogens. How many lone pairs are on the oxygen atom?
questions.hint: Oxygen has 6 valence electrons; after forming a double bond with C (4 shared electrons), how many remain?
questions.explanation: Oxygen has 6 valence electrons. In CH₂O, oxygen forms a double bond with carbon (shares 4 electrons). The remaining 4 electrons (2 lone pairs) are nonbonding. Thus oxygen has 2 lone pairs.

source_trace.content_anchor: Table 5.5 Lewis's formula of some simple covalent molecules: Formaldehyde

explanations_tiered.definition: A double bond shares 2 pairs of electrons (4 electrons). Lone pairs are nonbonding valence electrons.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: O atom in CH₂O has 6 valence e⁻. Double bond to C uses 4 e⁻ (2 pairs). Remaining 4 e⁻ form 2 lone pairs, giving O a complete octet.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Oxygen starts with 6 electrons. Sharing 4 with carbon (double bond) uses those, but it still needs 2 more to reach 8. So it keeps 2 pairs (4 electrons) for itself.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Lewis structure of CH₂O: C=O double bond. O has 2 lone pairs (4 e⁻) and 4 shared e⁻ in the double bond, total 8 e⁻.
explanations_tiered.advanced: In formaldehyde, oxygen is sp² hybridized with two lone pairs in sp² orbitals and one π bond to carbon. The lone pairs occupy the remaining sp² orbitals.
reasoning_steps: O has 6 valence e⁻.
C=O double bond uses 4 e⁻ (2 from O, 2 from C).
Remaining e⁻ on O = 6 − 2 (contributed to bond) = 4 e⁻.
4 e⁻ = 2 lone pairs.
misconceptions: Some think double bond means oxygen has no lone pairs. In CH₂O, O has two lone pairs.
Another misconception: oxygen in CH₂O has the same electron count as in H₂O. Both have 2 lone pairs, but bonding differs (double vs single bonds).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: CH₂O: O has double bond to C + 2 lone pairs.

glossary_entry.definition: CH₂O, a molecule with a carbon-oxygen double bond and two carbon-hydrogen single bonds.

flashcard.front: How many lone pairs are on oxygen in CH₂O?
flashcard.back: 2 lone pairs (oxygen has 6 valence e⁻; double bond uses 2 of its e⁻; remaining 4 e⁻ = 2 lone pairs).

concept_map_nodes.relation: has 6 valence e⁻ → double bond uses 2 e⁻ from O → remaining 4 e⁻ →

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How many lone pairs on oxygen in CH₂O?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: 2 lone pairs (double bond to C uses 2 of O's 6 valence e⁻; remaining 4 e⁻ = 2 lone pairs).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: CH₂O, a molecule with a carbon-oxygen double bond and two carbon-hydrogen single bonds.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Draw Lewis structure: H₂C=O with two lone pairs on O.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Draw Lewis structures for covalent molecules with double bonds

concept_map_nodes.relation: has 6 e⁻ → contributes 2 to double bond → 4 e⁻ remaining →

publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. O has 6 valence e⁻; double bond uses 2 e⁻ from O; remaining 4 e⁻ = 2 lone pairs. A (0 lone pairs) would give O only 4 e⁻ (double bond only). B (1 lone pair) would give O 6 e⁻. D (3 lone pairs) would exceed octet.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The hydronium ion (\(H_3O^+\)) forms when a water molecule bonds with a hydrogen ion (\(H^+\)). What is the correct description of the coordinate bond in \(H_3O^+\)?

options.A: Both electrons come from \(H^+\) and are shared with O
options.B: O donates a lone pair to \(H^+\); the bond is coordinate
options.C: O and \(H^+\) each donate one electron to form a normal covalent bond

questions.hint: Water has two lone pairs on oxygen; \(H^+\) has no electrons.
questions.explanation: Water (\(H_2O\)) has two lone pairs on oxygen. \(H^+\) is a proton with no electrons. Oxygen donates one lone pair to \(H^+\), forming a coordinate covalent bond. After formation, all O-H bonds are equivalent.

source_trace.content_anchor: Examples of such molecules include H2O, NH3, H2S. Examples of acceptors include H+

explanations_tiered.definition: A coordinate covalent bond forms when one atom provides both shared electrons.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: O in H₂O has two lone pairs. H⁺ has no electrons. O donates a lone pair to H⁺, forming a coordinate bond. The resulting H₃O⁺ has three equivalent O-H bonds.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Water has two 'private' electron pairs. A hydrogen ion has no electrons. Water shares one private pair with H⁺ — that's a coordinate bond.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Lewis base (H₂O) donates lone pair to Lewis acid (H⁺). The bond is coordinate because both electrons come from O, not one from each atom.
explanations_tiered.advanced: In H₃O⁺, the coordinate bond is identical to the other two O-H bonds after formation. The oxygen is sp³ hybridized with one lone pair remaining.
reasoning_steps: Identify donor: H₂O has lone pairs on O.
Identify acceptor: H⁺ has empty orbital, no electrons.
Donor (O) provides both electrons for the bond.
Therefore, the bond is coordinate (dative).
misconceptions: Some think H₃O⁺ has a normal covalent bond between O and H⁺. H⁺ has no electrons to contribute, so both electrons must come from O.
Another misconception: H₃O⁺ is ionic. The bond is covalent (coordinate), not ionic.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: H₃O⁺: O donates lone pair to H⁺ → coordinate bond.

glossary_entry.definition: \(H_3O^+\), formed by coordinate bonding between H₂O and H⁺.

flashcard.front: Describe the coordinate bond in H₃O⁺.
flashcard.back: O in H₂O donates a lone pair to H⁺ (which has no electrons).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How does H₃O⁺ form via coordinate bonding?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: O in H₂O donates a lone pair to H⁺ (acceptor), forming a coordinate covalent bond.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: \(H_3O^+\), formed by coordinate bonding between H₂O and H⁺.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: H₃O⁺: O donates lone pair to H⁺ (coordinate bond).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Relate to acid-base chemistry: H⁺ (acid) accepts lone pair from H₂O (base).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Elucidate formation of coordinate covalent bond using examples
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. O in H₂O donates a lone pair to H⁺ (B), forming a coordinate bond. A and D are false (H⁺ has no electrons to donate). C describes a normal covalent bond, which requires H⁺ to have an electron (it does not).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Gold (Au) and copper (Cu) are both excellent electrical conductors, but gold does not tarnish (react with oxygen) while copper forms a green patina (copper oxide) over time. What property of gold explains its resistance to tarnishing?

options.A: Gold has no delocalized electrons, so it cannot react.
options.B: Gold is very unreactive (low tendency to lose electrons) despite having delocalized electrons.
options.C: Gold forms a protective covalent layer that prevents reaction.
options.D: Gold has a very high melting point, so it does not react at room temperature.

questions.hint: Reactivity of metals depends on their tendency to lose electrons, not just the presence of delocalized electrons.
questions.explanation: Both gold and copper have delocalized electrons (metallic bonding). Gold's resistance to tarnishing is due to its low reactivity (noble metal). It has a very high ionization energy and does not readily lose electrons to form ions, unlike copper.

source_trace.content_anchor: Transition metals tend to have particularly high melting points and boiling points. The reason is that they can involve more valence electrons in the delocalization

explanations_tiered.definition: Noble metals (e.g., Au, Pt) have high resistance to oxidation due to high ionization energy and low reactivity.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Gold is a noble metal with high ionization energy; it does not readily lose electrons to form ions. Copper has lower ionization energy and reacts with oxygen over time. Both have delocalized electrons for conductivity.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Gold is a 'lazy' metal — it doesn't want to give away its electrons to oxygen. Copper is more eager to react, so it tarnishes.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Conductivity requires delocalized electrons (Au and Cu both have them). Reactivity depends on the ease of losing electrons (ionization energy). Au has a very high ionization energy (890 kJ/mol) compared to Cu (745 kJ/mol), making Au unreactive.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Gold's standard reduction potential (\(E° = +1.50 V\)) is much higher than copper's (+0.34 V), indicating Au³⁺ is very difficult to form. This thermodynamic stability prevents oxidation in air.
reasoning_steps: Recall that both Au and Cu are metals with delocalized electrons → conduct electricity.
Reactivity (tarnishing) depends on tendency to lose electrons.
Gold has very high ionization energy → does not lose electrons easily → no tarnish.
Copper has lower ionization energy → loses electrons to oxygen → tarnishes.
misconceptions: Some think all metals with delocalized electrons tarnish. Noble metals like gold do not tarnish due to high stability.
Another misconception: gold is unreactive because it has no free electrons. Gold has delocalized electrons (excellent conductor), but they are held tightly.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Au unreactive (high ionization energy); Cu tarnishes (lower ionization energy).

glossary_entry.definition: A metal that is highly resistant to oxidation and corrosion due to high ionization energy and low reactivity.

flashcard.front: Why does gold not tarnish while copper does?
flashcard.back: Gold has very high ionization energy (unreactive); copper has lower ionization energy and reacts with oxygen.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why is gold resistant to tarnishing while copper tarnishes?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Gold has high ionization energy (unreactive); copper has lower ionization energy and reacts with oxygen.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A metal that is highly resistant to oxidation and corrosion due to high ionization energy and low reactivity.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Au: high ionization energy → no tarnish. Cu: lower ionization energy → tarnishes.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Relate to the reactivity series of metals.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Relate metallic bonding to chemical reactivity
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Both Au and Cu have delocalized electrons (conductors), but Au has very high ionization energy (unreactive) while Cu has lower ionization energy (reacts with O₂). A is false (Au has delocalized e⁻). C is false (no protective layer). D is false (melting point not relevant to room temp tarnishing).
